1: šîr hamaʿălôt — "a song of the ascents" — the superscription that opens Ps 120 and heads each of the 15 psalms through Ps 134. maʿălôt (ascents, goings up, steps) from ʿālāh (to go up, ascend) — traditionally understood as songs sung by pilgrims ascending to Jerusalem for the three annual festivals (Deuteronomy 16:16), or as liturgical songs chanted on the fifteen steps of the temple. baṣṣārāh lî qārāʾtî ʾel-yhwh wayyaʿănēnî — "in my distress I cried to the LORD, and he answered me" — the psalm opens with a completed rescue, framing the distress as already heard. This retrospective structure is characteristic of thanksgiving psalms.
2: yhwh haṣṣîlāh napšî miśśĕpat-šāqer millāšôn rĕmiyyāh — "O LORD, rescue my life from lying lips, from a deceitful tongue" — two paired nouns: śĕpat-šāqer (lying/falsehood lip) and lāšôn rĕmiyyāh (tongue of deceit/treachery). rĕmiyyāh (deceit, slackness, treachery, from rāmāh, to deceive, throw) — the word carries active betrayal, not merely casual dishonesty. The pilgrim's first distress is not war or famine but the social violence of false speech in a hostile environment.
3: mah-yittēn lĕkā ûmah-yōsîp lāk lāšôn rĕmiyyāh — "what will be given to you, and what more will be done for you, O deceitful tongue?" — a taunt-address to the lying tongue itself. The rhetorical questions set up v4's answer. The verse form (what will be given? what will be added?) echoes the curse-formula of ancient Near Eastern treaty violations: the offending party is asked what they deserve before the punishment is declared.
4: ḥiṣṣê gibbôr šĕnûnîm ʿim gaḥălê rĕtāmîm — "sharp arrows of a mighty man, with burning coals of broom wood" — the answer to v3's question: the deceitful tongue deserves YHWH's sharpened arrows and the fierce burning coals of broom wood (rĕtāmîm). rōtem (broom shrub, Retama raetam) — a desert shrub; its charcoal burns with exceptional, sustained heat. The image evokes YHWH's warrior arsenal (Psalms 7:13; Psalms 18:14). The lying tongue has committed a war against the psalmist; it will be met with divine military response.
5: ʾôy-lî kî-gartî mešek šākantî ʿim-ʾohălê qēdār — "woe is me that I sojourn in Meshech, that I dwell among the tents of Kedar!" — Meshech appears in the Table of Nations (Genesis 10:2) as a son of Japheth; associated with Anatolia (modern Turkey). Kedar (Genesis 25:13) was a son of Ishmael; the Kedarites were semi-nomadic tribes of the Arabian desert. Together Meshech + Kedar represent the antipodes of the known world — the farthest possible distance from Jerusalem. This may be figurative for hostile pagans rather than geographic precision: the pilgrim feels exiled among those whose values are irreconcilably alien to covenant life.
6: rabbat šākenāh-llāh napšî ʿim śōnēʾ šālôm — "too long has my soul dwelt with those who hate peace" — rabbat (much, many, too long) — the accumulation of time among peace-haters has become intolerable. śōnēʾ šālôm (those who hate peace) — the compound noun-hater is not merely indifferent but actively hostile to šālôm (wholeness, well-being, covenant flourishing). This sets up the starkly personal antithesis of v7.
7: ʾănî-šālôm ûkî ʾădabbēr hēmmāh lammilḥāmāh — "I am peace (= for peace); but when I speak, they are for war" — the most compressed verse in the psalm. The Hebrew is jarring: ʾănî šālôm (I am peace) — the psalmist does not merely desire peace but identifies himself with it. The word order emphasizes the contrast: I = peace / they = war. The moment of speech becomes the flashpoint: the very act of speaking peace provokes war. The phrase anticipates Christ's beatitude on the peacemakers (Matthew 5:9) and Paul's description of the gospel as the "good news of peace" (Ephesians 6:15).
